items.prompt: Which requirement is the strongest signal for a backtracking search?

options.text: Enumerate every configuration that satisfies a set of constraints.
Compute one running sum over an array.
Find one value in a sorted array.
Count how many times each value appears.

feedback.reason: Backtracking is appropriate when a solution is assembled through a sequence of choices and multiple legal branches may need exploration.
feedback.details: Enumerating all constrained configurations naturally forms a choice tree. Each partial configuration defines the legal next choices, and the search restores branch-local state before exploring siblings.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_linear_aggregate: A deterministic aggregate has no branching choice tree.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_lookup: A monotonic lookup problem does not require exploring reversible choices.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_report: Frequency accumulation is direct state aggregation rather than search over alternatives.

items.prompt: A task asks for all length-k combinations chosen from n distinct values. Which structural description best matches the search?

options.text: A depth-k choice tree in which each level chooses the next value.
One deterministic left-to-right scan with no branching.
A binary search over sorted candidate values.
A frequency table containing all values.

feedback.reason: Each partial combination branches on the legal candidates for the next position.
feedback.details: The search depth is k. A start boundary prevents reuse and duplicate ordering, while each branch represents one possible next selected value.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_scan: A scan cannot represent all alternative subsets without branching.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_lookup: The task is not locating one monotonic boundary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_table: Frequency state does not enumerate different selections.

items.prompt: Which task is least likely to require backtracking?

options.text: Return the maximum value in an unsorted array.
Generate all permutations of distinct values.
Determine whether a word can be formed by a non-reusing path through a grid.
List all assignments satisfying pairwise constraints.

feedback.reason: A maximum can be computed by a deterministic linear scan with one running best value.
feedback.details: No choice must be revisited, undone, or explored in sibling branches. The other tasks naturally require searching alternatives.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_permutations: Permutation generation requires exploring many reversible choices.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.word_path: The path branches on neighboring cells and uses branch-local visited state.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.valid_assignments: Assignment construction forms a constrained choice tree.

items.prompt: A problem asks whether at least one valid assignment exists. Does the find-one result contract rule out backtracking?

options.text: No. Backtracking may stop as soon as one complete valid branch succeeds.
Yes. Backtracking is valid only when every solution must be returned.
Yes. Boolean results never require search.
Backtracking is allowed only when candidates are sorted.

feedback.reason: Backtracking describes how alternatives are explored, not whether the result contains one solution or all solutions.
feedback.details: A find-one solver can propagate success immediately. A collect-all solver instead records each complete branch and continues.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_all_only: Backtracking can support either find-one or collect-all contracts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.yes_no_output: A boolean can summarize the outcome of a branching search.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_if_sorted: Sorting may help duplicate control but is not a universal precondition.

items.prompt: Which detail most clearly distinguishes backtracking from an unrestricted brute-force loop over complete candidates?

options.text: The search maintains a valid partial state and rejects branches that cannot lead to a solution.
The input size is large.
The implementation uses recursion.
The code contains nested loops.

feedback.reason: Backtracking reasons about partial solutions, legal next choices, and restoration between branches.
feedback.details: It can still be exponential, but it is not merely unstructured enumeration: partial invariants and sound pruning shape the search tree.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.large_input: Input size alone does not define the algorithmic structure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.uses_recursion: Recursion can implement many non-backtracking algorithms.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.nested_loops: Loop nesting does not establish reversible branch exploration.

items.prompt: A string may be split at multiple positions, and every segment must satisfy a predicate. Which signal points to backtracking?

options.text: At each position, choose among multiple legal next segment boundaries.
Only compare each character with its neighbor.
Sort the eventual segments alphabetically.
Count the frequency of each character.

feedback.reason: Segmentation forms a tree whose edges are possible next cut positions.
feedback.details: Each branch commits to one segment, recursively solves the suffix, and then restores the path before trying another boundary.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_adjacent: Adjacent comparison does not enumerate segmentation alternatives.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_segments: Sorting output does not identify the search structure.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.count_characters: Character counts do not represent partition boundaries.

items.prompt: Which grid task has a backtracking signal rather than a standard flood-fill signal?

options.text: Find a path that spells a target word without reusing a cell in the same path.
Count connected components while each cell may be visited globally once.
Compute the sum of each row.
Find the smallest cell value.

feedback.reason: The word-path task explores alternative neighbor choices with visited state scoped to the current path.
feedback.details: A cell blocked in one candidate path may need to be available in a sibling path, so marking and undoing are central.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.count_components: Global reachability can use ordinary DFS or BFS without undoing visited state.

items.prompt: A problem asks for one shortest path in an unweighted graph. Which observation argues against choosing backtracking as the primary approach?

options.text: Layered breadth-first exploration directly guarantees the shortest number of edges.
The graph has more than one outgoing edge.
The result is a path.
The algorithm needs visited state.

feedback.reason: A specialized polynomial-time traversal should be preferred when it directly satisfies the objective.
feedback.details: Backtracking is not a default for every problem with choices. The goal and available invariants determine the appropriate search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.has_choices: Branching alone does not make backtracking the best method.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.uses_path: Many path problems are solved without backtracking search.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.needs_visited: Visited state appears in ordinary graph traversal too.

items.prompt: Which phrase is an unsafe reason by itself to classify a problem as backtracking?

options.text: "The solution uses recursion."
"The task asks for all valid outputs under constraints."
"Each branch makes a reversible choice and explores alternatives."
"A partial assignment can be rejected before completion."

feedback.reason: Recursion is an implementation mechanism, not a sufficient classification rule.
feedback.details: Tree traversal, divide-and-conquer, and dynamic programming can all be recursive without using choose-recurse-unchoose search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_valid_outputs: This is a strong backtracking signal.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.partial_constraints: This describes sound pruning in a choice tree.

items.prompt: A task generates all subsets of n distinct values. What is the branching decision at each input position in a common backtracking formulation?

options.text: Include the current value or exclude it.
Search the left half or the right half of a sorted array.
Increment or decrement the value.
Sort the input or leave it unsorted.

feedback.reason: Each position contributes a binary membership choice.
feedback.details: The resulting decision tree has depth n and up to 2^n leaves, one for each subset.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.left_or_right_half: That describes binary search, not subset construction.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.increment_or_decrement: The subset choice concerns membership, not modifying values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_or_not: Sorting is not the per-position decision that defines the subset tree.

items.prompt: A solver assigns one value to each variable and must satisfy constraints involving earlier assignments. What state transition suggests backtracking?

options.text: Assign a candidate, check consistency, recurse, then undo the assignment.
Add every candidate value into one running total.
Sort variables once and return the order.
Read one precomputed answer from a table.

feedback.reason: Variable assignment is a sequence of constrained choices whose effects must be isolated between branches.
feedback.details: Undo restores the partial assignment so sibling candidate values are evaluated from the same parent state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sum_once: A single aggregate does not explore alternative assignments.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_once: Sorting alone does not construct or test assignments.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lookup_once: A fixed lookup has no search transition.

items.prompt: Which result requirement most strongly suggests output-sensitive backtracking?

options.text: Return every valid permutation satisfying the constraints.
Return the input length.
Return the first array element.
Return a sorted copy of the input.

feedback.reason: When all solutions must be materialized, runtime must at least scale with the number and size of those outputs.
feedback.details: Backtracking can generate each valid leaf while sharing partial-path work across solutions.

items.prompt: Which features are strong backtracking signals? Select all that apply.

options.text: The algorithm builds a partial solution one decision at a time.
A state can have multiple legal next choices.
Mutable branch state must be restored before exploring a sibling.
Every input position has exactly one forced transition.

feedback.reason: Backtracking combines partial construction, branching, and branch-local state isolation.
feedback.details: Not every feature must appear in identical syntax, but the conceptual search tree and reversible decisions are central.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_deterministic_pass: A forced one-pass transition does not require branch exploration.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.partial_solution: Backtracking state is usually a partial candidate.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.multiple_legal_choices: Sibling choices form the branching structure.

items.prompt: Which tasks can naturally be modeled as backtracking choice trees? Select all that apply.

options.text: Generate all permutations of distinct values.
Assign values to variables subject to partial consistency constraints.
Enumerate all partitions whose segments satisfy a predicate.
Compute the sum of all array values.

feedback.reason: These tasks construct outputs by exploring alternative legal next choices.
feedback.details: The array sum is solved by one fixed accumulation path.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array_sum: A deterministic aggregate has no alternative branches.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.permutations: Each level chooses an unused next value.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.constrained_assignment: Each variable assignment is a branch choice.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.palindrome_partition: Each branch chooses the next segment boundary.

items.prompt: Which statements prevent the false heuristic that backtracking is merely unstructured brute force? Select all that apply.

options.text: Each recursion level has a defined partial-state invariant.
Candidate generation is limited to legal next choices.
A branch may be cut only when no completion can satisfy the contract.
Any branch that looks slow may be removed.

feedback.reason: Correct backtracking is governed by explicit state, choice, completion, and pruning contracts.
feedback.details: A branch is not removable merely because it appears inconvenient.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prune_unpromising: Performance intuition alone cannot preserve completeness.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.state_invariant: The invariant gives semantic meaning to the current node.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sound_pruning: Sound pruning is justified by impossibility.

items.prompt: A full choice tree has branching factor b and maximum depth d, with O(1) work per node besides recursion. Select an upper bound on visited nodes and recursion-stack space.
constraints: Every internal node may generate up to b children.

feedback.reason: The search tree can grow exponentially in depth, while depth-first recursion retains only one root-to-leaf path.
feedback.details: This bound is a structural warning, not proof that every backtracking problem reaches the full tree. Sound pruning and narrower candidate sets may reduce actual exploration.
